title: The “Clock” That Can Predict Death
summary: Dr. Steve Horvath, a UCLA aging researcher and biostatistician, discusses his groundbreaking work on epigenetic clocks, which use DNA methylation patterns to predict biological age and mortality risk. The interview explores how these clocks could revolutionize longevity research by enabling faster clinical trials and measuring the effectiveness of anti-aging interventions.

acronyms.definition: A type of blood collection tube used for drawing blood samples for epigenetic clock analysis
Regulatory body that approves research studies involving human subjects
Genetic markers used in genetic association studies

events.description: Dr. Steve Horvath created the pan tissue clock, the first epigenetic clock that applies to all human cell types and tissues

key_claims.claim: The pan tissue clock was the first epigenetic clock that applies to all human cell types, tissues, and organs
Epigenetic clocks can predict lifespan, time to heart disease, and other aging-related conditions
The GrimAge clock is named after the Grim Reaper because it is the best mortality risk predictor
A universal mammalian clock has been developed that applies to all species
Young plasma treatment showed over 50 percent reduction in epigenetic age in some rat tissues
Radiation like X-rays does not have an effect on methylation
DNA methylation rate is remarkably constant after age 20 in humans
Lifestyle choices like diet have a surprisingly weak effect on epigenetic aging
David Sinclair developed a protocol using gene modification that rejuvenated and re-grew the optic nerve for glaucoma treatment
Statistical genetics research over 10 years yielded disappointing results due to weak genetic signals
